title: Identical Bosons, large occupation numbers and classical field description
abstract: For a system with a large number of identical Bosons, it is common to claim, often without any additional justifications, that, when the mean occupation number in a single particle state is sufficiently large, classical field description will be applicable. This is why e.g. for ultra-light dark matter, the classical field equations are used to compute its dynamics. In this work, we test the validity and robustness of this assumption based on the criterion $2 σ_\varphi < |\langle \varphi \rangle| $ for classical field behaviour and applying it to aribtrary quantum states. We find that an arbitrary state with large occupation number doesn't behave classically while imposing some restrictions on the state vectors can improve the classical behavior. Since coherent states are known to have quasi-classical behaviour, we also ask how much deviation from coherent state can spoil the classical behaviour. Based on this analysis, we find that it is the proximity of the state to a large occupation coherent state rather than large occupation number itself which ensures validity of classical description. Implications of this for ultra light dark matter are discussed.
summary: This work rigorously tests the assumption that large occupation numbers automatically imply classical field behavior for Bosons. The authors demonstrate that this is false; instead, the validity of the classical description hinges on how closely the quantum state resembles a coherent state. This has direct implications for modeling phenomena like ultra-light dark matter.
main_problem: The paper investigates the conditions under which a quantum system of identical Bosons, characterized by a large mean occupation number, can be accurately described using classical field theory.
main_result: Classical behavior is not guaranteed merely by having a large occupation number; instead, it requires the quantum state to remain close to that of a coherent state.
method: The analysis uses theoretical diagnostics based on comparing the standard deviation ($\sigma_\varphi$) to the expectation value ($|\langle\varphiangle|$) using the criterion $2\sigma_\varphi < |\langle\varphiangle|$.
model_or_system: The system models non-relativistic, identical Bosons (relevant for ultra-light dark matter or Bose-Einstein Condensates) described by a quantum field operator $\hat{\varphi}(\mathbf{r})$. The state space is analyzed via its expansion in Fock states.
key_observables: Standard deviation ($\sigma_\varphi$), mean occupation number ($\langle Nangle$), and the ratio $\sigma_\varphi / |\langle\varphiangle|$.
important_parameters: The degree of deviation ($f$) from a coherent state, which governs the proximity to classicality.
main_assumptions: It is assumed that the Hamiltonian commutes with the total number operator ($\hat{H}$ commutes with $\hat{N}_{	ext{tot}}$) for simplicity in analyzing the state space structure.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how increasing the deviation of quantum coefficients from those defining a coherent state increases the ratio $\sigma_\varphi / |\langle\varphiangle|$, causing a breakdown of classical behavior.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the general problem and criterion for classicality. It then analyzes various types of quantum states (random, positive-coefficient, near-coherent) to test if large occupation number is sufficient. Finally, it discusses implications for physical systems like ultra-light dark matter.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical boundary condition for the semi-classical limit in many-body physics, which is crucial for interpreting cosmological simulations involving quantum fields (like wave DM).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: When the coefficients cn are uniformly distributed within (−1, 1), there is no correlation between the most quantities of our interest (except between σφ and ⟨N⟩in (c), with Pearson coefficient = 0.31 i.e. a weak correlation). In (d), the dashed line corresponds to σφ/|⟨φ⟩| for cns for a coherent state.

FIG. 2: When the coefficients cn are uniformly distributed within (0, 2), we begin to see weak correlations between the various quantities of our interest: the values of Pearson coefficient for the sub-figures being 0.55, -0.57, 0.20, -0.20 respectively. In (d), in addition to the dashed line for the case of coherent state, the solid line and dotted line correspond to σφ/|⟨φ⟩| = 1 and 1/2.

FIG. 3: Small and large deviations from coherent states: (a) |α|2 = 25, f = 0.1, (b), |α|2 = 25, f = 0.5, (c) |α|2 = 80, f = 2.0. As we increase the deviation from coherent state by increasing f, the ratio σφ/|⟨φ⟩| becomes larger and eventually, classical behaviour is lost.
